framework: A path qualifies only when >=3 prominent, proven people in the field visibly share the capability, each with a resolving link — the pattern across standouts is the evidence, not market demand. Ranked by strength of that pattern. Curated editorial, not DOL-derived.

paths.path: Reimplement landmark papers as clean, working open-source code
paths.what_it_is: Take a published paper and rebuild it from scratch as a small, readable, runnable codebase that others can learn from and build on, rather than only reading about it.
paths.why_it_signals: Karpathy, Wang, and Rush each built their reputations on exactly this craft — turning dense papers into approachable, working implementations — showing employers a rare combination of deep comprehension and production-grade engineering discipline.

paths.path: Publicly distill research into clear, widely-read technical writing
paths.what_it_is: Maintain a personal blog or long-form technical writeup practice that translates dense research (papers, model internals, new techniques) into explanations a broad technical audience can actually follow.
paths.why_it_signals: Weng, Alammar, and Olah each built long-running public writing practices that became must-read references in the field, demonstrating the rare ability to compress and clarify research — a skill labs value because it scales a scientist's impact far beyond their own team.

paths.path: Win competitive ML benchmarks and convert that track record into applied research
paths.what_it_is: Compete seriously on platforms like Kaggle — building and iterating rapidly on real predictive-modeling problems against a global field — and use the resulting track record and skills as evidence of applied research ability.
paths.why_it_signals: Deotte, Titericz, and Babakhin all reached senior applied/research scientist roles at NVIDIA specifically on the strength of Kaggle Grandmaster track records, showing a repeatable pattern where competitive-ML performance is read directly as applied-research signal by hiring teams.
